The person is a KodeKloud instructor and DevOps content creator planning a 50-minute live webinar titled "Agentic DevOps: The Playbook for Working Engineers" targeting working DevOps and Platform Engineers (primary) and Engineering Managers (secondary), scheduled for April 2026. The session is 50 minutes of presentation plus 10 minutes of Q&A. The conversation began from a compacted prior session that had already established the webinar scope, completed a LinkedIn promotional post, and set a tool-neutral positioning stance (tools as examples, not endorsements) appropriate for a KodeKloud audience.

The core work accomplished in this session was: (1) confirming the tool positioning as neutral/balanced rather than Claude/Anthropic-first; (2) commissioning and receiving a comprehensive Agentic DevOps landscape research report covering the state of the field as of March 2026, including confirmed production deployments, tool landscape, CNCF ecosystem, engineer role shifts, maturity models, and a 30/60/90-day adoption framework; (3) building a full webinar framework and narrative outline with section-by-section timing, key stats, and structural rationale; (4) expanding that into a complete 30-slide deck document with exact on-screen content and detailed speaker notes including cumulative timing cues, delivery direction, pause markers, and scripted Q&A responses; and (5) initiating the build of the actual PPTX file from those notes.

The 90-day plan targets moving organizations from maturity Level 1 to Level 2 with a starting use case of automated CI build failure analysis in advisory mode.

<parameter name="content"># DevOps Tool ROI: Balancing Initial Investment vs Long-Term Value

## The Bottom Line

DevOps tool investments typically break even within 12-18 months when properly planned and implemented. The key to maximizing ROI lies in strategic phasing, focusing on high-impact tools first, and measuring both direct cost savings and indirect productivity gains. Organizations that take a holistic approach to DevOps tooling see an average ROI of 300-400% over three years, with the most successful implementations following a build-versus-buy evaluation framework that prioritizes integration capabilities and long-term scalability.

## Understanding the True Cost of DevOps Tools

### Initial Investment Components

**Software Licensing and Subscriptions**
Most modern DevOps tools follow a subscription model, which can obscure the true long-term costs. Enterprise-grade tools typically range from $10-50 per user per month, but enterprise licenses often include volume discounts and multi-year commitments that can reduce costs by 20-40%.

**Implementation and Integration Costs**
The hidden expense that catches many organizations off-guard is integration work. Plan for implementation costs that equal 50-150% of your first-year licensing fees. This includes:
- Custom integrations between tools
- Data migration from legacy systems
- Security reviews and compliance validation
- Staff training and certification programs

**Infrastructure and Maintenance**
Cloud-hosted solutions reduce infrastructure overhead but increase ongoing operational costs. Self-hosted solutions require dedicated infrastructure, backup systems, and maintenance staff. Factor in 15-25% of licensing costs annually for infrastructure and maintenance.

### Ongoing Operational Expenses

**Training and Certification**
DevOps tools evolve rapidly. Budget $2,000-5,000 per team member annually for training, certification, and conference attendance to stay current with best practices and new features.

**Integration Maintenance**
APIs change, new features require integration updates, and security requirements evolve.</chat>

<parameter name="content"># AI-Enhanced DevOps: Transforming Infrastructure-as-Code with Generative AI
## 60-Minute Webinar (50 min presentation + 10 min Q&A)

---

## 🎯 **Webinar Description**

### **The Challenge: DevOps Teams Are Drowning in Configuration Complexity**

Modern DevOps teams spend 60-70% of their time writing and maintaining Infrastructure-as-Code (IaC) configurations—Terraform modules, Kubernetes manifests, Ansible playbooks, and Helm charts. This repetitive, error-prone work steals time from high-value architecture and optimization tasks.

**The result?** Slower deployments, configuration drift, security vulnerabilities, and burned-out engineers copying YAML from Stack Overflow at 2 AM.

### **The Solution: AI-Powered DevOps Workflows**

Discover how leading DevOps teams are using AI to **generate, review, and optimize** their infrastructure code—reducing configuration time by 80% while improving security and reliability.

In this hands-on webinar, you'll learn:
- **Practical AI integration strategies** for your existing DevOps workflows
- **Real-world case studies** from teams already seeing 10x productivity gains
- **Tool comparisons** and implementation roadmaps
- **Security and best practices** to avoid common AI pitfalls

input.title: The State of Agentic DevOps in Early 2026: Tools, Practices, and Governance for Autonomous AI in the Software Lifecycle
input.content:
# The state of Agentic DevOps in early 2026

**AI agents have crossed the threshold from coding assistants to autonomous pipeline participants, and the DevOps profession is restructuring around them.** Microsoft formally coined "Agentic DevOps" at Build 2025, and within ten months, every major cloud provider, observability vendor, and CI/CD platform has shipped agent-native capabilities. The distinction matters: AI-assisted DevOps (copilots, code suggestions) is synchronous and reactive; Agentic DevOps deploys goal-driven agents that plan, execute, and iterate across the full software lifecycle — often for hours without human intervention. For DevOps and platform engineers, the practical question is no longer whether to adopt AI agents, but where to start, what to trust, and how to govern systems that make their own decisions.

This report covers the landscape as of March 6, 2026, organized for a technically credible 50-minute webinar. Every claim is sourced, and speculative items are flagged.

---

## How "Agentic DevOps" got defined — and why it matters now

**Microsoft's Azure Blog (May 19, 2025) provided the industry's first formal definition:** *"The next evolution of DevOps, reimagined for a world where intelligent agents collaborate with you and with each other. The agents automate and optimize every stage of the software lifecycle."* GitHub CEO Thomas Dohmke described it as creating a "powerful Agentic DevOps loop" spanning development environments through production operations.

The industry has converged on a three-wave model of AI in development:

**Wave 1 — Pair Programmer (2021+):** Code completion inside the IDE. Synchronous, human-present for every keystroke. The original GitHub Copilot.

**Wave 2 — AI-Assisted (2023–2025):** Copilots offering chat-based assistance, architectural recommendations, vulnerability scanning. Still reactive and prompt-driven. This is where most teams are today.

**Wave 3 — Agentic (2025–present):** Autonomous agents that operate asynchronously, handle multi-step tasks, push commits independently, coordinate with other agents, and run for hours to days without constant oversight.

The critical distinction is operational scope. AI-assisted tools live in the "inner loop" — writing code inside an editor. Agentic DevOps targets the **"outer loop"** — CI/CD, deployment, monitoring, incident response, and cross-tool handoffs where delivery actually stalls. Opsera's research (February 2026) quantified this bottleneck precisely: AI-assisted workflows produced PRs **48–58% faster**, but those AI-generated PRs waited **4.6x longer** to be reviewed, contained **13.5% vs 10.5% code duplication**, and introduced **15–18% more security vulnerabilities**. The outer loop became the new constraint, and agentic systems are the industry's response.

Several forces converged to trigger this shift. LLM capabilities matured to sustain multi-step reasoning and tool use (Anthropic's Opus 4.6 handles **14.5-hour task horizons**). Anthropic's **Model Context Protocol (MCP)** became the de facto integration standard, enabling agents to connect to external tools and APIs — ThoughtWorks Tech Radar Vol. 33 called it "the ultimate integration protocol for powering agents." Kubernetes reached **82% production adoption** (CNCF 2025), providing a mature runtime for agent workloads. And economic pressure accelerated everything: CNCF reports **56% of organizations are understaffed** on platform engineers.

### Where the analysts stand

**Gartner** predicts **40% of enterprise apps** will integrate task-specific AI agents by end of 2026, up from less than 5% in early 2025. They project 70% of enterprises deploying agentic AI in IT infrastructure operations by 2029. Their five-stage evolution model runs from Assistants (2025) through Task-Specific Agents (2026) to Collaborative Ecosystems (2028+). Gartner also reports a **1,445% surge** in multi-agent system inquiries from Q1 2024 to Q2 2025.

**Forrester** takes a more cautious stance. Their 2026 predictions forecast that **fewer than 15% of firms** will actually enable agentic features in intelligent automation suites this year. VP Diego Lo Giudice defined "Agentic Software Development" on March 2, 2026, and announced a Forrester Wave evaluation of ASD tools for H2 2026. Their top 2026 prediction: a **major breach caused by agentic AI** with insufficient governance guardrails.

**ThoughtWorks Tech Radar Vol. 33** (late 2025) devoted nearly half its blips to AI, placed Claude Code in the Trial ring, and emphasized MCP security risks: *"When APIs are naively exposed to agents via MCP, there's no reliable, deterministic way to prevent an autonomous AI agent from misusing such endpoints."*

---

## What's actually in production vs. still experimental

The gap between AI *assistants* (widely deployed) and AI *agents* (beginning to deploy) remains significant. Here is what is confirmed in production, what is in preview, and what remains aspirational.

### Confirmed production deployments

**Elastic** integrated Claude Code into their Buildkite CI pipelines for their cloud control plane monorepo (~500 actively updated dependencies). When dependency-update PRs break the build, a Claude-powered agent autonomously analyzes errors, proposes code fixes, commits changes, and restarts the pipeline. Elastic described it as "so successful that it started helping our teams before becoming a fully polished internal feature." This is one of the clearest documented agentic CI/CD implementations.

**Datadog's Bits AI SRE Agent** reached general availability in December 2025 after soft-launching in June. It runs autonomous incident investigations across **2,000+ customer production environments** — forming hypotheses, querying logs/metrics/traces, and producing root-cause analyses. Datadog claims up to 95% reduction in time-to-resolution (a ceiling figure, not an average; vendor-reported).

**Block (Square)** built a PagerDuty MCP extension connecting their open-source AI agent "Goose" with incident management workflows. Senior Software Engineer Will Pfleger described it as "a production tool that automates triage and root cause analysis across our teams" — a homegrown integration demonstrating how engineering teams are building custom agentic pipelines.

**GitHub Copilot's Coding Agent** (GA September 2025) has created over **1 million pull requests** between May–September 2025. Copilot code review improved developer effectiveness for 72.6% of surveyed users. **90% of Fortune 100 companies** now use Copilot.

**Spotify** reports up to **90% reduction in engineering time** and **650+ AI-generated code changes shipped per month** through Claude-powered systems.

### In preview or early access (not yet GA)

**AWS DevOps Agent** (public preview, January 2026) uses a multi-agent architecture where a lead agent acts as "incident commander," delegating to specialized sub-agents. Integrates telemetry from CloudWatch, Datadog, New Relic, and Splunk with deployment history from GitHub/GitLab CI/CD. AWS's own blog acknowledged: *"Graduating that prototype into a product that performs reliably across diverse customer environments is a different challenge entirely."*

**Azure SRE Agent** (preview, announced Build 2025) monitors, diagnoses, and resolves issues in Azure-hosted apps. Integrates with PagerDuty, Datadog, New Relic, and Dynatrace via MCP connectors.

**PagerDuty's SRE Agent** (GA target October 2025) learns from past incidents, recommends diagnostics, and generates self-updating runbooks. Customers report resolving incidents "up to 50% faster" (vendor-claimed).

### The DORA paradox

The **2025 DORA Report** (Google Cloud, ~5,000 respondents) found that **90% of respondents use AI at work**, with a median of **2 hours/day** interacting with AI tools. AI adoption now positively correlates with delivery throughput (reversed from a negative 2024 finding), but **still negatively correlates with delivery stability** — meaning more change failures. The report's key insight: *"AI doesn't fix a team; it amplifies what's already there."* Teams should expect AI agents to accelerate both good and bad practices.

### Most common entry points (in adoption order)

Teams are adopting agentic capabilities in a clear progression: (1) AI code generation and completion (near-universal; 46% of active Copilot users' code is AI-generated); (2) AI-assisted code review and PR management (GitHub Copilot has auto-reviewed 8M+ PRs); (3) intelligent incident triage and root cause analysis (the most *agentic* use case in production today — Datadog, PagerDuty, AWS, Azure); (4) AI-powered test selection and optimization; and (5) automated remediation and self-healing (most nascent, typically still requiring human-in-the-loop approval).

---

## The tools and platforms landscape as of March 2026

### Claude Code

Claude Code runs as a **terminal-based agentic coding assistant** powered by **Opus 4.6** (released February 5, 2026), which features a **1-million-token context window** and the industry's longest autonomous task horizon at **14.5 hours** (METR measurement). Anthropic projects **$500M+ annualized revenue** from Claude Code, a 10x increase from early 2025.

For DevOps teams, the most consequential recent feature is **Claude Code Security** (February 20, 2026) — an AI-powered security auditor that scans entire repositories for complex, context-specific vulnerabilities. During internal testing it found **500+ real vulnerabilities** in production open-source projects. Available as a limited research preview for Enterprise/Team customers.

Claude Code supports **MCP integration**, a **skills system** with 72 plugins and 112 agents in the marketplace, **multi-agent parallel execution** (engineers report running 6+ agents simultaneously on cloud instances), and AGENTS.md files for per-repo agent behavior configuration. It integrates with CI/CD via the Anthropic API, Amazon Bedrock, and Google Vertex AI. The New York Stock Exchange is building internal AI agents using the **Claude Agent SDK** that take instructions from Jira tickets through to committed code.

In February 2026, Anthropic demonstrated **16 Claude Opus 4.6 agents** building a C compiler (~100K lines of Rust) autonomously over two weeks, successfully compiling the Linux kernel, PostgreSQL, SQLite, and Redis — at ~$20K in API costs and ~2 billion input tokens.

### GitHub Copilot and Agent HQ

Copilot Workspace was sunset May 30, 2025. Its capabilities were rebuilt into the **Copilot Coding Agent** (GA September 2025), which operates in three modalities: an async cloud-based agent (assign issues to `@copilot`, get draft PRs), a sync IDE-based Agent Mode (VS Code, JetBrains, Eclipse, Xcode), and a CLI agent with specialized sub-agents for exploration and task execution.

**Agent HQ** (announced GitHub Universe, October 2025) is the control plane for orchestrating multiple coding agents. In early 2026, it expanded to include **Claude by Anthropic and OpenAI Codex** alongside Copilot — developers can assign the same issue to Copilot, Claude, and Codex simultaneously and compare approaches. It supports **multi-model selection** including GPT-5.1-Codex-Max, Claude Opus 4.5, Gemini 3 Pro, Grok Code Fast 1, and Bring-Your-Own-Key for enterprises. Custom agents are definable at `.github/agents/`.

### OpenAI Codex

The original Codex API (2021–2023) is long deprecated. "Codex" was **relaunched in May 2025** as a cloud-based software engineering agent — a fundamentally different product. The current model is **GPT-5.3-Codex** (with GPT-5.4 announced March 5, 2026), described as "the most capable agentic coding model to date" and the first model "instrumental in creating itself." It can work independently for **7+ hours** on complex tasks.

For DevOps integration, Codex ships a **GitHub Action** for CI/CD pipeline integration, a **TypeScript SDK** (`@openai/codex-sdk`) for embedding agents in custom workflows, **Automations** for unprompted work on issue triage and CI/CD tasks, and a **Slack integration** where tagging `@Codex` triggers autonomous task completion. Codex is used by **70% of OpenAI engineers internally**, who report merging 70% more PRs per week.

### LangGraph

**LangGraph 1.0** reached GA in late 2025 — the first stable major release in the durable agent framework space, with **47M+ PyPI downloads** making it the most adopted AI agent framework. It provides graph-based orchestration where nodes represent agents/functions/decision points and edges define data flow. Key capabilities for DevOps include **durable execution** (agent state persists through failures), first-class **human-in-the-loop** APIs for pausing and approving agent actions, built-in memory for cross-session context, and MCP integration via streamable HTTP.

DevOps teams use LangGraph for planner-executor patterns (planner breaks down incident response into steps, executor performs each one), backend agents automating workflows across monitoring/ticketing/deployment tools, and multi-agent orchestration with integration into shell, SQL, Python, and API toolkits. Enterprise adopters include **Uber, LinkedIn, Klarna, and Ally Financial**. The steep learning curve (requires understanding state machines and async programming) and fast-evolving codebase are documented challenges.

### Argo Workflows as agent execution platform

**There is no official Argo project support for AI agent workloads** as of March 2026. Argo Workflows remains a Kubernetes-native workflow engine (CNCF Graduated) for DAG and step-based parallel job orchestration. Teams *can* use it to run containerized AI agent tasks as workflow steps, and it is widely used for ML pipeline orchestration alongside Kubeflow and Airflow. The Hera Python SDK makes it more accessible for Python-based AI teams. But this is a **custom integration pattern, not a built-in capability** — Argo provides the execution substrate, not agent-specific primitives. ArgoCon Amsterdam 2026 (March 23–26, colocated with KubeCon EU) may surface new community patterns. *[Flag: Argo as an "agent execution platform" is technically accurate but potentially misleading — it's a general-purpose workflow engine that happens to work for agent orchestration, not a purpose-built agent platform.]*

### Other breakout tools

**Dagger** (v0.18+) has shipped **experimental LLM primitives** with automatic function discovery, multi-provider support (OpenAI, Anthropic, Google), and controlled environments for AI agents. The "Pipeline Doctor" pattern — CI failure → agent analysis → agent heal — is their marquee agentic use case. SDKs in 8 languages. Performance gains of 5–6x reported by OpenMeter and Civo.

**Encore Cloud 2.0** ("Development Platform for the AI Era") features "Leap," an AI developer agent, plus a **local MCP server** allowing AI agents deep introspection into databases, APIs, services, and traces. An Agent Skills package works with Cursor, Claude Code, GitHub Copilot, and 10+ other agents.

**Pulumi Neo** (public preview September 2025) is positioned as "the industry's first AI-powered platform engineer." It accepts natural-language infrastructure commands with three progressive autonomy modes: Review (everything needs approval), Balanced (only deployments need approval), and Auto (full autonomy). Werner Enterprises reported reducing provisioning from **3 days to 4 hours** while maintaining SOC 2 compliance. AGENTS.md support was added February 6, 2026.

**CrewAI** is the fastest-growing multi-agent framework with **100,000+ developers** certified. MIT-licensed, role-based orchestration with YAML-driven configuration. DevOps use cases include incident detection, ticket creation, and dashboard updates. **AutoGen** (Microsoft Research) is now in **maintenance mode** — bug fixes only.

Notable startups: **Mendral** (AI DevOps Engineer for CI failures, flaky tests, broken releases), **OneGrep** (DevOps agent across observability/CI-CD/infra), **Opslane** (developer-first K8s delivery without YAML), **Rootly** (AI-powered incident management with self-healing), and **SRE.ai** ($7.2M raised, "Virtual Employee Economics" for SRE).

---

## The engineer's role is restructuring around agent boundaries

The shift from "DevOps engineer who uses AI tools" to "engineer who designs agentic systems" is now visible in job postings, conference talks, and practitioner frameworks. DevOps.com identifies three emerging sub-roles: the **escalatory role** (handling issues agents can't), the **management role** (overseeing agents like a team), and the **AgenticOps role** (operating at the intersection of AI and operations).

### Five skills DevOps engineers need now

**Context engineering** has overtaken prompt engineering as the critical new skill. Patrick Debois (who coined "DevOps") stated at an InfoQ panel that DevOps engineers "are in the best position because they know how to make things work in the cloud" — and urged engineers to build custom MCP servers connecting observability tools directly to AI agents.

**Agent evaluation** follows eval-driven development (analogous to TDD). AWS's blog on building the DevOps Agent (January 2026) outlines five critical mechanisms: evaluations (using LLM judges and pass@k metrics), trajectory visualization (OpenTelemetry-based), fast feedback loops, intentional changes, and production sampling of agent runs.

**Multi-agent architecture design** requires understanding delegation patterns. AWS's DevOps Agent uses a lead agent as "incident commander" that creates investigation plans and delegates to specialized sub-agents, each receiving "a pristine context window" to prevent context pollution.

**Policy-as-code for agent governance** means defining agent permissions, blast radius constraints, and approval gates in code. Practitioners recommend creating separate service principals per agent function — a generation agent with read-only access, a validation agent that can run `terraform plan` but not `apply`, and a deployment agent requiring human approval gates.

**AI supply chain security** emerged as critical after Snyk's "ToxicSkills" research (February 2026) found that **13.4% of 3,984 publicly registered AI agent skills** had critical vulnerabilities, with 76 confirmed malicious payloads. Engineers must evaluate agent skill supply chains with the same rigor as package dependencies.

### What "designing agent boundaries" means in practice

This phrase refers to architectural decisions about what an agent can see, do, and affect. An AWS practitioner documented: *"The IAM role an agent assumes is its blast radius. When AI agents operate inside your cloud environment, IAM design becomes architecture, not just access control."* Concretely, it means separating input validation/rate limiting from core agent reasoning (like API gateways in front of microservices), enforcing multi-validation layers where no single check can pass a hallucinated output, and designing context window boundaries so sub-agents don't get overwhelmed by noise.

### Real role changes in the market

Active job postings (March 2026) include: **Microsoft — AI Platform Engineer** (AI Product Acceleration team), **Zoom — AI Engineer, Agent Automation** (requiring "agent orchestrator design, agent workflow, and LLM evaluation metrics"), **Tesla — Software Engineer, Generative AI Platform Engineering**, and **The Hartford — AI Platform Engineer (GCP)**. Indeed listings show "AI Platform Engineer" and "AI Engineer - Agent Automation" as standardized titles. A DEV Community analysis declares *"The 'DevOps Engineer' is Dead. Long Live the Platform Architect"* — though this overstates the current reality.

**Kelsey Hightower** offers important skepticism. At Civo Navigate 2025: *"An AI agent is software. Its only purpose is to deal with the data. So, we're back to square one."* And: *"The security people don't know what their role is... The operations people have no idea what their role is."* His core advice: fundamentals still apply, and engineers should encode best practices into platforms so "the next developers don't need to keep relearning operational expertise."

---

## Kubernetes is becoming the agent runtime — here's what that looks like

Kubernetes now hosts **66% of organizations' AI inference workloads** (CNCF 2026), and a rich ecosystem of CRDs, operators, and CNCF projects has emerged specifically for agentic workloads.

### Deployment patterns and key projects

The **CRD + Operator pattern** dominates. **Kagent** (CNCF Sandbox, contributed by Solo.io) is the first Kubernetes-native agent framework — an `Agent` CRD defines system prompt, tools, and LLM configuration; a controller watches CRDs and creates pod resources. It ships pre-built agents for Kubernetes, Helm, Istio, Cilium, Prometheus/Grafana, and Argo Rollouts. **Agent Sandbox** (kubernetes-sigs, subproject of SIG Apps, launched by Google at KubeCon NA 2025) provides a new `Sandbox` CRD for isolated agent code execution with gVisor or Kata Containers backends, designed to scale to tens of thousands of parallel sandboxes. **Kagenti** (Red Hat incubation, v0.2.0-alpha.21) implements SPIFFE-based cryptographic identity for agents and Google's A2A (Agent-to-Agent) protocol.

The **sidecar pattern** is implemented by **Dapr Agents** (CNCF Graduated project). Dapr's sidecar injects pub/sub, state management, secrets, and workflow capabilities alongside agent containers, with virtual actors providing lightweight stateful agent instances (thousands per core, ~50ms activation). Dapr Workflows provide **durable execution** — every agent interaction persists to a state store (28+ backends: Postgres, Redis, DynamoDB, etc.), enabling recovery after restarts.

For autoscaling, **KEDA** (CNCF Graduated) scales agent pods to zero when idle and up based on demand. Agent Sandbox's WarmPool extension maintains pre-warmed pods for **sub-second cold start latency**.

### Agent observability is coalescing around OpenTelemetry

The **OpenTelemetry GenAI SIG** is actively defining semantic conventions for AI agents, with initial conventions based on Google's AI Agent white paper. Agent-specific metrics include reasoning loop counts, tool call success/failure rates, token usage and cost per invocation, and loop/stall detection — fundamentally different from traditional request latency metrics.

Commercial tools have shipped ahead of open standards. **Datadog's LLM Observability** (GA June 2025) includes an AI Agents Console with interactive execution flow charts visualizing agent decision paths, and auto-instruments the OpenAI Agent SDK, LangGraph, CrewAI, and Bedrock Agent SDK. **New Relic's Agentic AI Monitoring** (preview, expanded February 2026) provides an Agents Service Map visualizing inter-agent interactions and AI Trace Views showing every step with latency and errors. **Grafana Assistant** (GA October 2025) serves as a context-aware AI agent inside Grafana Cloud, with 10x user growth in 90 days.

### Security requires a new stack

The fundamental challenge, as articulated by Tigera's Ratan Tipirneni: *"Agents are autonomous and non-deterministic. You cannot predict which agents are going to talk to other agents or what actions they will take."*

The emerging security stack combines **SPIFFE/SPIRE** (CNCF Graduated) for cryptographic workload identity — each agent pod receives an automatically rotated short-lived X.509 SVID instead of long-lived service account tokens — with **OPA/Gatekeeper or Kyverno** for admission policy enforcement on agent pods, and **Agent Sandbox** (gVisor/Kata Containers) for process, storage, and network isolation of untrusted agent code. Dapr provides built-in circuit breakers, retries, and timeouts. The OWASP 2026 Top 10 for Agentic Systems emphasizes that *"execution must be isolated with minimal blast radius"* and that inter-agent communication must follow **Zero Trust principles**.

### CNCF ecosystem alignment

KubeCon NA 2025 (Atlanta) launched the **Certified Kubernetes AI Conformance Program** standardizing DRA, gang scheduling, volume handling, and job-level networking for AI workloads (v2.0 planned for 2026). KubeCon EU 2026 (Amsterdam, March 23–26) features a dedicated Cloud Native & Kubernetes AI Day with sessions including "Enterprise-Scale Migrations Using Agentic Workflows with Human-in-the-loop" from Adobe. The CNCF **WG Serving** (concluded February 2026) focused on inference gateway patterns, while **SIG Apps** hosts Agent Sandbox and **WG Artificial Intelligence** under TAG Runtime meets biweekly.

---

## A practical framework for getting started

### The maturity spectrum

Based on multiple sources — Coder's 5-Stage AI Maturity Curve (January 2026), Ali Arsanjani's Agentic AI Maturity Model, IDC FutureScape 2026, and Microsoft's framework — a synthesized maturity model emerges:

- **Level 0 — Manual:** No AI in DevOps workflows. Traditional CI/CD, manual debugging.
- **Level 1 — AI-Assisted:** Engineers use Copilot/ChatGPT individually for code generation, troubleshooting. Ad hoc, ungoverned.
- **Level 2 — AI-Augmented:** AI integrated into pipeline stages. Automated code review, AI-powered test selection, advisory mode with human approval.
- **Level 3 — Agent-Embedded:** AI agents operate within defined boundaries. Multi-agent architectures, policy-as-code guardrails, agent observability, eval suites.
- **Level 4 — Autonomous (Governed):** Self-healing, self-optimizing systems with sandboxed autonomous operation, constitutional guardrails, kill switches, and continuous production sampling.

IDC estimates only **1% of organizations** have reached Level 4 maturity, with over half still at Levels 0–1.

### A realistic 30/60/90 day plan

**Days 1–30 (Foundation):** Start with a single, low-risk use case in a non-production environment — the best entry point is automated analysis of CI build failures or dependency-update PRs (following Elastic's pattern). Audit your data quality, existing APIs, and integration points. Set up an "Agentic AI Pod" (2–3 engineers). Define agent permissions using separate service principals with least-privilege access. Begin building your eval suite immediately — AWS recommends this as day-one work.

**Days 31–60 (Validation):** Implement multi-layer validation: no single check should be able to pass a hallucinated output. Add agent observability (Datadog LLM Observability, New Relic AI Monitoring, or open-source Langfuse). Track agent actions, success rates, and false positives. Run agents in advisory mode where they recommend actions but require human approval. Create long-running evaluation environments to test agent behavior under varied conditions.

**Days 61–90 (Controlled expansion):** Gradually increase agent autonomy as confidence builds based on eval data. Expand to production incident triage support (not remediation). Sample production agent runs regularly with trajectory visualization. Begin cross-team knowledge sharing and documentation. Define cadence for prompt tuning and workflow rollout.

### The failure modes that kill adoption

**Hallucination propagation** is the most dangerous technical failure. IBM warns: *"When agents use hallucinated details in DevOps workflows, they can quietly propagate errors through the codebase and automation pipelines, where they compound and cause cascading failures."* One study found developers take **20% longer** to resolve issues when using AI that introduces subtle errors.

**Retry loops on ambiguous failures** — an agent without a hard retry budget will keep spinning on flaky tests indefinitely. Explicit retry budgets and circuit breakers are essential.

**State drift between sessions** — an agent builds a pipeline with full context, but six weeks later during incident diagnosis, it must reconstruct context from logs and config. Without investment in audit trails during the build phase, diagnostic sessions become slower than human alternatives.

**Deploying organization-wide on day one** is the most common organizational failure. Multiple sources confirm this "guarantees failures, erodes trust, and creates resistance that's hard to overcome later."

**Gartner predicts** that by end of 2027, more than **40% of agentic AI projects will fail or be canceled** due to escalating costs, unclear business value, or insufficient risk controls. *[Flag: This is a forward-looking prediction, not observed data.]*

---

## What just happened: the last 90 days at the cutting edge

The period from December 2025 through March 2026 has been the most consequential window yet for Agentic DevOps. Here are the developments that matter most for working engineers.

**AWS re:Invent (December 2025)** unveiled three "Frontier Agents": **Kiro Autonomous Agent** (works independently for days, runs 10 concurrent tasks — Amazon claims a 30-developer/18-month project was completed by 6 developers in 76 days using Kiro internally), **AWS Security Agent**, and **AWS DevOps Agent**. CEO Matt Garman: *"This is not just the 10 to 20 percent efficiency gains... This is orders of magnitude more efficient."* *[Flag: Vendor claim without independent verification.]*

**Anthropic** shipped Claude Code Security (February 20, 2026), causing cybersecurity stocks to drop (CrowdStrike fell ~8%). They demonstrated 16 agents building a C compiler autonomously. And they launched **Agent Skills** — reusable packages of instructions, scripts, and resources for IaC, CI/CD, and runbook automation.

**OpenAI** released **GPT-5.3-Codex** (February 5, 2026) and **GPT-5.4** (March 5, 2026), with the Codex app expanding to Windows with parallel agents, isolated worktrees, and CI/CD automations.

**Google** launched **Gemini 3** (February 2026) for advanced agentic operations and massively expanded the **Agent Development Kit (ADK)** (February 27, 2026) with GitHub, GitLab, Postman, and observability tool integrations. **Gemini CLI** went open source with MCP support and 1M token context.

**GitHub's Agent HQ** expanded to include Claude, Codex, and custom agents — developers can now assign the same issue to multiple agents simultaneously and compare their approaches. This is the clearest signal that multi-agent orchestration has moved from framework concept to platform feature.

**CNCF** is hosting KubeCon EU 2026 in Amsterdam (March 23–26) with **224 sessions** including dedicated AI and platform engineering tracks. The Kubernetes AI Conformance program announced v2.0 targeting advanced inference patterns and stricter security requirements.

### Where forward-looking practitioners say this goes next

The consensus among thought leaders is a shift from "can we build agents?" to "can we govern them at scale?" IBM declared: *"The proof-of-concept phase is over. In 2026, the challenge isn't whether agentic AI works — it's whether you can deploy it reliably across your organization, at scale."* Infosys's Global Head of AI Sunil Senan predicts: *"By 2026, AIOps will evolve from dashboards and recommendations to agentic AI that can autonomously diagnose, act and verify fixes... Human operators will increasingly move into a supervisory role."* Multiple sources emphasize that **MCP mastery** is becoming the critical DevOps skill — Descope's Rishi Bhargava: *"DevOps should focus on mastering MCP, which is set to create an entirely new app development pipeline in 2026."*

The market is projected to grow fast. Deloitte estimates the autonomous AI agent market could reach **$8.5 billion by 2026** and $35 billion by 2030.

---

## Conclusion

Agentic DevOps is real, shipping, and measurably impacting production workflows at companies like Elastic, Block, and Spotify — but the industry is still between Level 1 (AI-Assisted) and Level 2 (AI-Augmented) on the maturity curve, with fully autonomous operations remaining rare and risky. The most credible entry points are **automated CI failure analysis** and **AI-assisted incident triage**, not end-to-end autonomous pipelines. The tool ecosystem has reached a critical mass where Claude Code, GitHub Copilot's Coding Agent, and OpenAI Codex offer genuinely agentic capabilities in CI/CD, while LangGraph and Dapr provide the orchestration substrate. Argo Workflows serves as a general-purpose execution layer but lacks agent-specific primitives — it's infrastructure, not framework.

For DevOps engineers, the career-defining skill shift is from writing automation to designing agent boundaries — defining what agents can see, do, and affect through IAM architecture, eval suites, context engineering, and policy-as-code. The engineers who thrive will be those who treat agents as they treat any distributed system: with observability, governance, blast radius constraints, and healthy skepticism about vendor claims of autonomy. Start small (one non-production CI use case), build your eval suite on day one, and resist the organizational pressure to deploy everywhere at once. The tooling is ready. The governance is not. That gap is where the real engineering work lives.

# Agentic DevOps: The Playbook for Working Engineers
### KodeKloud Live Webinar — 50 Minutes + 10 Min Q&A
**Target Audience:** Working DevOps/Platform Engineers (primary), Engineering Managers (secondary)
**Core Thesis:** Your existing skills aren't being replaced. A new layer is being added on top — and you're already positioned to own it.

---

## Narrative Arc

```
WHY NOW → WHAT IT IS → WHAT'S REAL → HOW YOU FIT → WHERE TO START
```

This isn't a tools demo. It's a decision framework. Every section answers a question the audience is actually asking.

---

## Section Breakdown

### SECTION 1 — The Signal in the Noise (5 min)
*Hook: Why this conversation is different from the last 10 "AI will change DevOps" talks*

**Opening beat:** The Boris Cherny / Claude Code moment.
- Anthropic's Claude Code is writing 100% of Anthropic's own code
- Anthropic still has 100+ open engineering positions
- The Head of Claude Code said: someone has to prompt the models, talk to customers, coordinate teams, decide what to build next
- That's not a reassurance — that's a job description for what DevOps engineers are about to become

**The real shift happening right now (March 2026):**
- GitHub Copilot has created 1M+ pull requests autonomously since May 2025
- Elastic's CI pipeline now self-heals when builds break — a Claude agent analyzes failures, commits fixes, restarts the pipeline
- Datadog's Bits AI SRE Agent is doing autonomous incident investigation across 2,000 production environments
- AWS, Google, Microsoft all shipped autonomous agent products at their December 2025 conferences — in the same week

**The honest framing:**
- This is NOT replacing DevOps engineers
- This IS restructuring what those engineers spend their time on
- The engineers who miss the shift are the ones who wait to be told what to do about it

**Transition:** Before we talk about what to do — we need to define what we're actually talking about

---

### SECTION 2 — What Is Agentic DevOps, Actually (8 min)
*Clarity: Cutting through the hype with a concrete definition and a framework that sticks*

**The three waves (2 min):**

| Wave | What it looks like | Where most teams are |
|------|-------------------|----------------------|
| Wave 1: Pair Programmer (2021+) | Code completion in the IDE. You're present for every keystroke. GitHub Copilot v1. | Done |
| Wave 2: AI-Assisted (2023–2025) | Chat-based help, code review, vulnerability scans. Reactive. You ask, it answers. | Most teams today |
| Wave 3: Agentic (2025–present) | Autonomous agents that operate for hours without oversight. They plan, execute, iterate, and hand off to other agents. | Beginning now |

**The critical distinction — inner loop vs. outer loop (2 min):**
- AI-Assisted tools live in the **inner loop** — writing code inside an editor
- Agentic DevOps targets the **outer loop** — CI/CD, deployment, monitoring, incident response
- This matters because: AI-assisted tools made PRs 48% faster, but those PRs waited 4.6x longer to be reviewed (Opsera 2026)
- The bottleneck moved from writing code to everything around it
- That outer loop is exactly where DevOps engineers already live

**The honest definition (1 min):**
> Agentic DevOps: autonomous, goal-driven AI systems operating within your existing infrastructure — Kubernetes, CI/CD, observability tooling — that can plan, execute, and iterate on multi-step tasks without constant human direction.

**What it is NOT (1 min):**
- It is not magic
- It is not a replacement for understanding your systems
- It is not safe to deploy without governance
- Forrester predicts a major breach caused by agentic AI with insufficient guardrails will happen this year — this is a real risk

**The maturity reality check (2 min):**
- Level 0: No AI in DevOps
- Level 1: Engineers using AI tools individually, ad hoc
- Level 2: AI integrated into pipeline stages with human approval
- Level 3: Agents operating within defined boundaries with policy guardrails
- Level 4: Autonomous, self-healing, self-optimizing systems

IDC estimates only **1% of organizations** are at Level 4. Most are at Level 1. The opportunity is Level 2 → Level 3.

---

### SECTION 3 — What's Real Right Now (10 min)
*Credibility: What's actually in production vs. what's still a demo*

**Frame this section:** "I'm not going to tell you what vendors say is possible. I'm going to tell you what's confirmed shipping."

**Confirmed in production — 4 case studies (6 min):**

**1. Elastic — Self-healing CI/CD**
- 500+ actively updated dependencies in a cloud control plane monorepo
- When dependency-update PRs break builds, a Claude-powered agent analyzes the error, proposes a fix, commits it, restarts the pipeline
- Elastic: "So successful it started helping our teams before becoming a polished internal product"
- *What this means for you:* This is a reproducible pattern. Build failure analysis is the #1 recommended starting point for any team.

**2. Datadog — AI SRE Agent (GA December 2025)**
- Runs autonomous incident investigations across 2,000 production environments
- Forms hypotheses, queries logs/metrics/traces, produces root cause analysis
- Claims up to 95% reduction in time-to-resolution (ceiling number, not average — but the pattern is real)
- *What this means for you:* Incident triage is the second confirmed high-ROI agentic use case in production today.

**3. Block (Square) — PagerDuty + Goose agent**
- Built their own MCP extension connecting their open-source "Goose" agent with incident management
- Production tool automating triage and RCA across engineering teams
- Built by their engineers, not bought as a product
- *What this means for you:* Platform engineers who can build MCP integrations are the ones defining how agentic systems work at their companies.

**4. Spotify — AI-generated code changes**
- 650+ AI-generated code changes shipped per month via Claude-powered systems
- Up to 90% reduction in engineering time on targeted workflows
- *What this means for you:* Volume and speed are real at scale. But this required significant internal platform investment to get there.

**The tools actually worth knowing right now (4 min):**

**Claude Code**
- Terminal-based agentic coding assistant running on Opus 4.6
- 1M token context window, 14.5-hour autonomous task horizon
- MCP integration, skills marketplace (72 plugins, 112 agents)
- February 2026: Claude Code Security — scanned open-source repos and found 500+ real vulnerabilities
- Used by NYSE to build agents that take Jira tickets to committed code autonomously

**GitHub Copilot Coding Agent + Agent HQ**
- Assign issues to `@copilot`, get draft PRs back — no IDE required
- Agent HQ (October 2025) lets you run Claude, Codex, and Copilot on the same issue and compare results
- 90% of Fortune 100 companies now using Copilot
- 46% of active users' code is now AI-generated

**OpenAI Codex (the new one — not the old API)**
- Relaunched May 2025, completely different product — GPT-5.3-Codex as of February 2026
- 7+ hour autonomous task horizon
- GitHub Action for CI/CD pipeline integration
- 70% of OpenAI engineers use it internally; report merging 70% more PRs per week

**LangGraph 1.0 (GA late 2025)**
- Most adopted AI agent framework — 47M+ PyPI downloads
- Graph-based orchestration: nodes are agents, edges are data flow
- Durable execution (state persists through failures), built-in human-in-the-loop APIs
- Used by Uber, LinkedIn, Klarna, Ally Financial
- The framework to know if you're building custom agentic workflows

**Argo Workflows — honest assessment**
- Not an agent platform. A workflow engine.
- You *can* use it to run agent tasks as workflow steps
- But it has no agent-specific primitives — you're bringing your own orchestration logic
- If you're already using Argo, it's a valid execution substrate. If you're not, don't adopt it just for agents.

---

### SECTION 4 — The Role Shift: What Engineers Actually Need to Know (12 min)
*The core of the webinar: practical skills framework*

**The honest job description change (2 min):**

Old version of the DevOps engineer's core skill:
> "Automate everything. Write the scripts. Own the pipelines."

New version:
> "Design the boundaries. Define what agents can see, do, and affect. Explain why the system made the decisions it made."

This is not a demotion. It's an architectural promotion. The pipeline engineer becomes the platform architect.

**The 5 skills you need to build right now (8 min):**

**Skill 1: Context Engineering**
- Prompt engineering was Wave 2. Context engineering is Wave 3.
- Agents don't just respond to prompts — they operate from context: AGENTS.md files, MCP server configurations, memory stores, tool access definitions
- Patrick Debois (the person who coined "DevOps") said: *"DevOps engineers are in the best position because they know how to make things work in the cloud"* — and urged engineers to build custom MCP servers connecting your observability tools directly to agents
- Practical action: Learn MCP. Build one MCP server. Connect your most-used tool.

**Skill 2: Agent Boundary Design**
- What an agent can access = its blast radius
- This is IAM architecture at a new level: a code generation agent gets read-only access; a validation agent can run `terraform plan` but not `apply`; a deployment agent needs a human approval gate
- Snyk's February 2026 research found 13.4% of 3,984 public AI agent skills had critical vulnerabilities — 76 had confirmed malicious payloads
- You evaluate agent skills the same way you evaluate package dependencies
- Practical action: For any agent you deploy, write down: what can it read, write, execute, and who approves irreversible actions?

**Skill 3: Evaluation (Evals) — the new testing discipline**
- This is eval-driven development. Same discipline as TDD, different artifacts.
- AWS's published framework for production agents: evaluations using LLM judges + pass@k metrics, trajectory visualization with OpenTelemetry, fast feedback loops, and production sampling
- You don't deploy an agent to production that you haven't evaluated against realistic failure scenarios
- Practical action: Before any agent goes to CI, build a small eval suite against your 10 most common failure modes.

**Skill 4: Agent Observability**
- Traditional metrics: latency, error rate, throughput
- Agent-specific metrics: reasoning loop counts, tool call success/failure rates, token usage and cost per invocation, stall detection
- The tooling is here: Datadog LLM Observability (GA June 2025), New Relic Agentic AI Monitoring (expanded February 2026), Grafana Assistant (GA October 2025)
- OpenTelemetry GenAI SIG is standardizing semantic conventions right now — watch this
- Practical action: Add agent observability before you add agent autonomy. You can't govern what you can't see.

**Skill 5: Multi-Agent Architecture Design**
- Agents working alone are limited. Agents working together are powerful and risky.
- The production pattern: lead agent as "incident commander" → delegates to specialized sub-agents → each gets a clean context window (no pollution from other agents' reasoning)
- AWS's DevOps Agent uses exactly this pattern — planner, executor, validator as separate agents
- LangGraph's graph model makes this explicit and auditable
- Practical action: Don't start with multi-agent. Master single-agent first. Add coordination only when you have a specific reason.

**What the failure modes actually look like (2 min):**

1. **Hallucination propagation** — agent introduces a subtle error early in a workflow, subsequent agents trust it, it compounds silently. One study: developers take 20% longer to resolve issues introduced by AI than they do to solve original problems.

2. **Retry loops** — agent hits an ambiguous failure and retries indefinitely. No retry budget = infinite loop.

3. **State drift** — agent builds something with full context, six weeks later another agent (or the same one) diagnoses it with no context. No audit trail = slower than human investigation.

4. **Deploying everywhere on day one** — multiple sources confirm this is the single most common failure pattern. It erodes trust company-wide and is very hard to walk back.

---

### SECTION 5 — Your 90-Day Getting Started Playbook (10 min)
*The takeaway: leave with a real plan, not just awareness*

**The maturity target (1 min):**
You're probably at Level 1 (individual AI tool use). The goal for the next 90 days is reaching Level 2 (AI embedded in pipelines with human approval gates) for at least one workflow. That's it. Not Level 4. Not autonomous systems. Level 2.

**Days 1–30: Foundation (3 min)**

Pick ONE use case. The proven entry point is **automated CI build failure analysis** — when a build breaks, an agent analyzes the error and recommends a fix (advisory mode, human approves the commit). Why this one:
- Low blast radius (read-only in most implementations)
- Immediate visible value
- Exact pattern Elastic used to ship their first production agent

Parallel work:
- Set up your eval suite first (before the agent touches real pipelines)
- Define IAM/permissions for the agent — separate service principal, read-only to start
- Form a 2–3 person "Agentic AI Pod" — you need at least one person who owns this

Tools to evaluate this month: Claude Code (for agent implementation), LangGraph or Dapr (for orchestration), MCP (for tool connectivity)

**Days 31–60: Validation (3 min)**

Agents in advisory mode only. They recommend, humans approve.

- Add agent observability before you increase autonomy — Datadog LLM Observability, New Relic, or open-source Langfuse
- Track: agent action accuracy rate, false positives, time saved vs. time reviewing agent recommendations
- Run your eval suite against CI failure scenarios weekly
- If the eval numbers look good and humans are agreeing with agent recommendations >70% of the time, you're ready to expand
- If they're not — tune context, not just prompts

**Days 61–90: Controlled Expansion (3 min)**

One additional use case: **AI-assisted incident triage** (agent surfaces relevant logs/metrics/traces and produces a hypothesis — human confirms before any remediation).

- Agents still in advisory mode for remediation
- Agents can autonomously surface data (read-only)
- Begin documenting what your agents have touched — this becomes your audit trail
- Share learnings across teams — not as a mandate, as a story

What NOT to do in 90 days:
- Don't give agents write access to production before 60 days minimum
- Don't skip the eval suite ("we'll add tests later" = you won't)
- Don't let the vendor demo be your eval — vendor claims are ceiling numbers

**The question your CTO is going to ask (1 min):**

> "Why did the agent make that decision?"

If you can't answer that, you're not ready to deploy. If you can answer it — because you have trajectory logs, eval suites, and observability in place — you're ahead of 99% of teams doing this right now.

---

### CLOSING — The Real Opportunity (5 min)
*Re-anchor to the thesis. Leave them with a specific action.*

**The uncomfortable truth about timing:**

The DORA 2025 report found that AI adoption now positively correlates with delivery throughput — but still negatively correlates with delivery stability. More AI = more change failures, on average, at teams that deploy carelessly.

The engineers who will define what Agentic DevOps looks like — who will write the patterns, set the governance standards, build the internal platforms — are working on it right now. Not in two years when there's a certification for it. Now.

Your Kubernetes knowledge? That's the runtime agents operate on.
Your CI/CD expertise? That's the pipeline agents are embedded in.
Your observability skills? That's the only way to govern what agents do.

You are not behind. You are positioned. The question is whether you start building this layer in the next 90 days or watch someone else define it for your organization.

**One concrete action before you leave this call:**

Go to your CI pipeline. Find the last 5 build failures. Ask yourself: could an agent have analyzed these and recommended a fix — with you approving the commit? If yes, you have your first use case. Start there.

**Registration link / KodeKloud resource / whatever CTA applies**

---

### Q&A — 10 Min

**Anticipated questions and honest answers:**

*"Isn't this going to eliminate junior DevOps roles?"*
Honest answer: Some entry-level tasks are being absorbed. The engineers who grow fast are the ones learning to supervise and design these systems, not just use them. KodeKloud content is specifically designed to develop that layer of skill.

*"Which tools should I learn first?"*
Claude Code or GitHub Copilot Coding Agent for hands-on agent use. LangGraph for understanding orchestration. MCP for connectivity. Pick one. Don't try to learn all three at once.

*"What's the security risk I'm actually taking on?"*
The blast radius of your IAM design is the security risk. Snyk found 13% of public agent skills have critical vulnerabilities. Treat agents like you treat any third-party code: review permissions, don't give them more than they need, monitor what they touch.

*"Our org isn't going to let agents touch production. Is this still relevant?"*
Yes. Start in dev/staging environments. Build the eval suite. Demonstrate the advisory-mode value. That's how you make the case to move forward.

---

## Timing Summary

| Section | Content | Time |
|---------|---------|------|
| 1 | The Signal in the Noise | 5 min |
| 2 | What Is Agentic DevOps, Actually | 8 min |
| 3 | What's Real Right Now | 10 min |
| 4 | The Role Shift & 5 Skills | 12 min |
| 5 | 90-Day Getting Started Playbook | 10 min |
| Close | Real Opportunity + CTA | 5 min |
| Q&A | | 10 min |
| **Total** | | **60 min** |

---

## Slide Count Estimate
~28–32 slides total (not counting title/agenda/Q&A slides)
- Section 1: 4 slides
- Section 2: 6 slides
- Section 3: 8 slides
- Section 4: 8 slides
- Section 5: 4 slides
- Close: 2 slides

---

## Key Stats to Have Ready (for credibility)
- 1M+ PRs created autonomously by GitHub Copilot Coding Agent (May–Sept 2025)
- 46% of active Copilot users' code is now AI-generated
- Opsera: AI-assisted PRs created 48% faster but waited 4.6x longer to be reviewed
- Datadog Bits AI: autonomous incident investigation across 2,000 production environments
- Spotify: 650+ AI-generated code changes/month, up to 90% engineering time reduction on targeted workflows
- DORA 2025: 90% of respondents use AI at work, median 2 hours/day
- IDC: Only 1% of organizations at full autonomous maturity (Level 4)
- Gartner: 40% of enterprise apps will integrate task-specific agents by end of 2026
- Snyk: 13.4% of public agent skills had critical vulnerabilities (February 2026)
- Anthropic Claude Opus 4.6: 14.5-hour autonomous task horizon (METR measurement)

- Agents don't just respond to prompts — they operate from context: AGENTS.md files, MCP server configurations, memory stores, tool access definitions
- Patrick Debois (the person who coined "DevOps") said: *"DevOps engineers are in the best position because they know how to make things work in the cloud"* — and urged engineers to build custom MCP servers connecting your observability tools directly to agents
- Practical action: Learn MCP. Build one MCP server. Connect your most-used tool.

**Skill 2: Agent Boundary Design**
- What an agent can access = its blast radius
- This is IAM architecture at a new level: a code generation agent gets read-only access; a validation agent can run `terraform plan` but not `apply`; a deployment agent needs a human approval gate
- Snyk's February 2026 research found 13.4% of 3,984 public AI agent skills had critical vulnerabilities — 76 had confirmed malicious payloads
- You evaluate agent skills the same way you evaluate package dependencies
- Practical action: For any agent you deploy, write down: what can it read, write, execute, and who approves irreversible actions?

**Skill 3: Evaluation (Evals) — the new testing discipline**
- This is eval-driven development. Same discipline as TDD, different artifacts.
- AWS's published framework for production agents: evaluations using LLM judges + pass@k metrics, trajectory visualization with OpenTelemetry, fast feedback loops, and production sampling
- You don't deploy an agent to production that you haven't evaluated against realistic failure scenarios
- Practical action: Before any agent goes to CI, build a small eval suite against your 10 most common failure modes.

**Skill 4: Agent Observability**
- Traditional metrics: latency, error rate, throughput
- Agent-specific metrics: reasoning loop counts, tool call success/failure rates, token usage and cost per invocation, stall detection
- The tooling is here: Datadog LLM Observability (GA June 2025), New Relic Agentic AI Monitoring (expanded February 2026), Grafana Assistant (GA October 2025)
- OpenTelemetry GenAI SIG is standardizing semantic conventions right now — watch this
- Practical action: Add agent observability before you add agent autonomy. You can't govern what you can't see.

**Skill 5: Multi-Agent Architecture Design**
- Agents working alone are limited. Agents working together are powerful and risky.
- The production pattern: lead agent as "incident commander" → delegates to specialized sub-agents → each gets a clean context window (no pollution from other agents' reasoning)
- AWS's DevOps Agent uses exactly this pattern — planner, executor, validator as separate agents
- LangGraph's graph model makes this explicit and auditable
- Practical action: Don't start with multi-agent. Master single-agent first. Add coordination only when you have a specific reason.

**What the failure modes actually look like (2 min):**

1. **Hallucination propagation** — agent introduces a subtle error early in a workflow, subsequent agents trust it, it compounds silently. One study: developers take 20% longer to resolve issues introduced by AI than they do to solve original problems.

2. **Retry loops** — agent hits an ambiguous failure and retries indefinitely. No retry budget = infinite loop.

3. **State drift** — agent builds something with full context, six weeks later another agent (or the same one) diagnoses it with no context. No audit trail = slower than human investigation.

4. **Deploying everywhere on day one** — multiple sources confirm this is the single most common failure pattern. It erodes trust company-wide and is very hard to walk back.

---

### SECTION 5 — Your 90-Day Getting Started Playbook (10 min)
*The takeaway: leave with a real plan, not just awareness*

**The maturity target (1 min):**
You're probably at Level 1 (individual AI tool use). The goal for the next 90 days is reaching Level 2 (AI embedded in pipelines with human approval gates) for at least one workflow. That's it. Not Level 4. Not autonomous systems. Level 2.

**Days 1–30: Foundation (3 min)**

Pick ONE use case. The proven entry point is **automated CI build failure analysis** — when a build breaks, an agent analyzes the error and recommends a fix (advisory mode, human approves the commit). Why this one:
- Low blast radius (read-only in most implementations)
- Immediate visible value
- Exact pattern Elastic used to ship their first production agent

Parallel work:
- Set up your eval suite first (before the agent touches real pipelines)
- Define IAM/permissions for the agent — separate service principal, read-only to start
- Form a 2–3 person "Agentic AI Pod" — you need at least one person who owns this

Tools to evaluate this month: Claude Code (for agent implementation), LangGraph or Dapr (for orchestration), MCP (for tool connectivity)

**Days 31–60: Validation (3 min)**

Agents in advisory mode only. They recommend, humans approve.

- Add agent observability before you increase autonomy — Datadog LLM Observability, New Relic, or open-source Langfuse
- Track: agent action accuracy rate, false positives, time saved vs. time reviewing agent recommendations
- Run your eval suite against CI failure scenarios weekly
- If the eval numbers look good and humans are agreeing with agent recommendations >70% of the time, you're ready to expand
- If they're not — tune context, not just prompts

**Days 61–90: Controlled Expansion (3 min)**

One additional use case: **AI-assisted incident triage** (agent surfaces relevant logs/metrics/traces and produces a hypothesis — human confirms before any remediation).

- Agents still in advisory mode for remediation
- Agents can autonomously surface data (read-only)
- Begin documenting what your agents have touched — this becomes your audit trail
- Share learnings across teams — not as a mandate, as a story

What NOT to do in 90 days:
- Don't give agents write access to production before 60 days minimum
- Don't skip the eval suite ("we'll add tests later" = you won't)
- Don't let the vendor demo be your eval — vendor claims are ceiling numbers

**The question your CTO is going to ask (1 min):**

> "Why did the agent make that decision?"

If you can't answer that, you're not ready to deploy. If you can answer it — because you have trajectory logs, eval suites, and observability in place — you're ahead of 99% of teams doing this right now.

---

### CLOSING — The Real Opportunity (5 min)
*Re-anchor to the thesis. Leave them with a specific action.*

**The uncomfortable truth about timing:**

The DORA 2025 report found that AI adoption now positively correlates with delivery throughput — but still negatively correlates with delivery stability. More AI = more change failures, on average, at teams that deploy carelessly.

The engineers who will define what Agentic DevOps looks like — who will write the patterns, set the governance standards, build the internal platforms — are working on it right now. Not in two years when there's a certification for it. Now.

Your Kubernetes knowledge? That's the runtime agents operate on.
Your CI/CD expertise? That's the pipeline agents are embedded in.
Your observability skills? That's the only way to govern what agents do.

You are not behind. You are positioned. The question is whether you start building this layer in the next 90 days or watch someone else define it for your organization.

**One concrete action before you leave this call:**

Go to your CI pipeline. Find the last 5 build failures. Ask yourself: could an agent have analyzed these and recommended a fix — with you approving the commit? If yes, you have your first use case. Start there.

**Registration link / KodeKloud resource / whatever CTA applies**

---

### Q&A — 10 Min

**Anticipated questions and honest answers:**

*"Isn't this going to eliminate junior DevOps roles?"*
Honest answer: Some entry-level tasks are being absorbed. The engineers who grow fast are the ones learning to supervise and design these systems, not just use them. KodeKloud content is specifically designed to develop that layer of skill.

*"Which tools should I learn first?"*
Claude Code or GitHub Copilot Coding Agent for hands-on agent use. LangGraph for understanding orchestration. MCP for connectivity. Pick one. Don't try to learn all three at once.

*"What's the security risk I'm actually taking on?"*
The blast radius of your IAM design is the security risk. Snyk found 13% of public agent skills have critical vulnerabilities. Treat agents like you treat any third-party code: review permissions, don't give them more than they need, monitor what they touch.

*"Our org isn't going to let agents touch production. Is this still relevant?"*
Yes. Start in dev/staging environments. Build the eval suite. Demonstrate the advisory-mode value. That's how you make the case to move forward.

---

## Timing Summary

| Section | Content | Time |
|---------|---------|------|
| 1 | The Signal in the Noise | 5 min |
| 2 | What Is Agentic DevOps, Actually | 8 min |
| 3 | What's Real Right Now | 10 min |
| 4 | The Role Shift & 5 Skills | 12 min |
| 5 | 90-Day Getting Started Playbook | 10 min |
| Close | Real Opportunity + CTA | 5 min |
| Q&A | | 10 min |
| **Total** | | **60 min** |

---

## Slide Count Estimate
~28–32 slides total (not counting title/agenda/Q&A slides)
- Section 1: 4 slides
- Section 2: 6 slides
- Section 3: 8 slides
- Section 4: 8 slides
- Section 5: 4 slides
- Close: 2 slides

---

## Key Stats to Have Ready (for credibility)
- 1M+ PRs created autonomously by GitHub Copilot Coding Agent (May–Sept 2025)
- 46% of active Copilot users' code is now AI-generated
- Opsera: AI-assisted PRs created 48% faster but waited 4.6x longer to be reviewed
- Datadog Bits AI: autonomous incident investigation across 2,000 production environments
- Spotify: 650+ AI-generated code changes/month, up to 90% engineering time reduction on targeted workflows
- DORA 2025: 90% of respondents use AI at work, median 2 hours/day
- IDC: Only 1% of organizations at full autonomous maturity (Level 4)
- Gartner: 40% of enterprise apps will integrate task-specific agents by end of 2026
- Snyk: 13.4% of public agent skills had critical vulnerabilities (February 2026)
- Anthropic Claude Opus 4.6: 14.5-hour autonomous task horizon (METR measurement)

1	---
     2	name: pptx
     3	description: "Use this skill any time a .pptx file is involved in any way — as input, output, or both. This includes: creating slide decks, pitch decks, or presentations; reading, parsing, or extracting text from any .pptx file (even if the extracted content will be used elsewhere, like in an email or summary); editing, modifying, or updating existing presentations; combining or splitting slide files; working with templates, layouts, speaker notes, or comments. Trigger whenever the user mentions \"deck,\" \"slides,\" \"presentation,\" or references a .pptx filename, regardless of what they plan to do with the content afterward. If a .pptx file needs to be opened, created, or touched, use this skill."
     4	license: Proprietary. LICENSE.txt has complete terms
     5	---
     6	
     7	# PPTX Skill
     8	
     9	## Quick Reference
    10	
    11	| Task | Guide |
    12	|------|-------|
    13	| Read/analyze content | `python -m markitdown presentation.pptx` |
    14	| Edit or create from template | Read [editing.md](editing.md) |
    15	| Create from scratch | Read [pptxgenjs.md](pptxgenjs.md) |
    16	
    17	---
    18	
    19	## Reading Content
    20	
    21	```bash
    22	# Text extraction
    23	python -m markitdown presentation.pptx
    24	
    25	# Visual overview
    26	python scripts/thumbnail.py presentation.pptx
    27	
    28	# Raw XML
    29	python scripts/office/unpack.py presentation.pptx unpacked/
    30	```
    31	
    32	---
    33	
    34	## Editing Workflow
    35	
    36	**Read [editing.md](editing.md) for full details.**
    37	
    38	1. Analyze template with `thumbnail.py`
    39	2. Unpack → manipulate slides → edit content → clean → pack
    40	
    41	---
    42	
    43	## Creating from Scratch
    44	
    45	**Read [pptxgenjs.md](pptxgenjs.md) for full details.**
    46	
    47	Use when no template or reference presentation is available.
    48	
    49	---
    50	
    51	## Design Ideas
    52	
    53	**Don't create boring slides.** Plain bullets on a white background won't impress anyone. Consider ideas from this list for each slide.
    54	
    55	### Before Starting
    56	
    57	- **Pick a bold, content-informed color palette**: The palette should feel designed for THIS topic. If swapping your colors into a completely different presentation would still "work," you haven't made specific enough choices.
    58	- **Dominance over equality**: One color should dominate (60-70% visual weight), with 1-2 supporting tones and one sharp accent. Never give all colors equal weight.
    59	- **Dark/light contrast**: Dark backgrounds for title + conclusion slides, light for content ("sandwich" structure). Or commit to dark throughout for a premium feel.
    60	- **Commit to a visual motif**: Pick ONE distinctive element and repeat it — rounded image frames, icons in colored circles, thick single-side borders. Carry it across every slide.
    61	
    62	### Color Palettes
    63	
    64	Choose colors that match your topic — don't default to generic blue. Use these palettes as inspiration:
    65	
    66	| Theme | Primary | Secondary | Accent |
    67	|-------|---------|-----------|--------|
    68	| **Midnight Executive** | `1E2761` (navy) | `CADCFC` (ice blue) | `FFFFFF` (white) |
    69	| **Forest & Moss** | `2C5F2D` (forest) | `97BC62` (moss) | `F5F5F5` (cream) |
    70	| **Coral Energy** | `F96167` (coral) | `F9E795` (gold) | `2F3C7E` (navy) |
    71	| **Warm Terracotta** | `B85042` (terracotta) | `E7E8D1` (sand) | `A7BEAE` (sage) |
    72	| **Ocean Gradient** | `065A82` (deep blue) | `1C7293` (teal) | `21295C` (midnight) |
    73	| **Charcoal Minimal** | `36454F` (charcoal) | `F2F2F2` (off-white) | `212121` (black) |
    74	| **Teal Trust** | `028090` (teal) | `00A896` (seafoam) | `02C39A` (mint) |
    75	| **Berry & Cream** | `6D2E46` (berry) | `A26769` (dusty rose) | `ECE2D0` (cream) |
    76	| **Sage Calm** | `84B59F` (sage) | `69A297` (eucalyptus) | `50808E` (slate) |
    77	| **Cherry Bold** | `990011` (cherry) | `FCF6F5` (off-white) | `2F3C7E` (navy) |
    78	
    79	### For Each Slide
    80	
    81	**Every slide needs a visual element** — image, chart, icon, or shape. Text-only slides are forgettable.
    82	
    83	**Layout options:**
    84	- Two-column (text left, illustration on right)
    85	- Icon + text rows (icon in colored circle, bold header, description below)
    86	- 2x2 or 2x3 grid (image on one side, grid of content blocks on other)
    87	- Half-bleed image (full left or right side) with content overlay
    88	
    89	**Data display:**
    90	- Large stat callouts (big numbers 60-72pt with small labels below)
    91	- Comparison columns (before/after, pros/cons, side-by-side options)
    92	- Timeline or process flow (numbered steps, arrows)
    93	
    94	**Visual polish:**
    95	- Icons in small colored circles next to section headers
    96	- Italic accent text for key stats or taglines
    97	
    98	### Typography
    99	
   100	**Choose an interesting font pairing** — don't default to Arial. Pick a header font with personality and pair it with a clean body font.
   101	
   102	| Header Font | Body Font |
   103	|-------------|-----------|
   104	| Georgia | Calibri |
   105	| Arial Black | Arial |
   106	| Calibri | Calibri Light |
   107	| Cambria | Calibri |
   108	| Trebuchet MS | Calibri |
   109	| Impact | Arial |
   110	| Palatino | Garamond |
   111	| Consolas | Calibri |
   112	
   113	| Element | Size |
   114	|---------|------|
   115	| Slide title | 36-44pt bold |
   116	| Section header | 20-24pt bold |
   117	| Body text | 14-16pt |
   118	| Captions | 10-12pt muted |
   119	
   120	### Spacing
   121	
   122	- 0.5" minimum margins
   123	- 0.3-0.5" between content blocks
   124	- Leave breathing room—don't fill every inch
   125	
   126	### Avoid (Common Mistakes)
   127	
   128	- **Don't repeat the same layout** — vary columns, cards, and callouts across slides
   129	- **Don't center body text** — left-align paragraphs and lists; center only titles
   130	- **Don't skimp on size contrast** — titles need 36pt+ to stand out from 14-16pt body
   131	- **Don't default to blue** — pick colors that reflect the specific topic
   132	- **Don't mix spacing randomly** — choose 0.3" or 0.5" gaps and use consistently
   133	- **Don't style one slide and leave the rest plain** — commit fully or keep it simple throughout
   134	- **Don't create text-only slides** — add images, icons, charts, or visual elements; avoid plain title + bullets
   135	- **Don't forget text box padding** — when aligning lines or shapes with text edges, set `margin: 0` on the text box or offset the shape to account for padding
   136	- **Don't use low-contrast elements** — icons AND text need strong contrast against the background; avoid light text on light backgrounds or dark text on dark backgrounds
   137	- **NEVER use accent lines under titles** — these are a hallmark of AI-generated slides; use whitespace or background color instead
   138	
   139	---
   140	
   141	## QA (Required)
   142	
   143	**Assume there are problems. Your job is to find them.**
   144	
   145	Your first render is almost never correct. Approach QA as a bug hunt, not a confirmation step. If you found zero issues on first inspection, you weren't looking hard enough.
   146	
   147	### Content QA
   148	
   149	```bash
   150	python -m markitdown output.pptx
   151	```
   152	
   153	Check for missing content, typos, wrong order.
   154	
   155	**When using templates, check for leftover placeholder text:**
   156	
   157	```bash
   158	python -m markitdown output.pptx | grep -iE "\bx{3,}\b|lorem|ipsum|\bTODO|\[insert|this.*(page|slide).*layout"
   159	```
   160	
   161	If grep returns results, fix them before declaring success.
   162	
   163	### Visual QA
   164	
   165	**⚠️ USE SUBAGENTS** — even for 2-3 slides. You've been staring at the code and will see what you expect, not what's there. Subagents have fresh eyes.
   166	
   167	Convert slides to images (see [Converting to Images](#converting-to-images)), then use this prompt:
   168	
   169	```
   170	Visually inspect these slides. Assume there are issues — find them.
   171	
   172	Look for:
   173	- Overlapping elements (text through shapes, lines through words, stacked elements)
   174	- Text overflow or cut off at edges/box boundaries
   175	- Decorative lines positioned for single-line text but title wrapped to two lines
   176	- Source citations or footers colliding with content above
   177	- Elements too close (< 0.3" gaps) or cards/sections nearly touching
   178	- Uneven gaps (large empty area in one place, cramped in another)
   179	- Insufficient margin from slide edges (< 0.5")
   180	- Columns or similar elements not aligned consistently
   181	- Low-contrast text (e.g., light gray text on cream-colored background)
   182	- Low-contrast icons (e.g., dark icons on dark backgrounds without a contrasting circle)
   183	- Text boxes too narrow causing excessive wrapping
   184	- Leftover placeholder content
   185	
   186	For each slide, list issues or areas of concern, even if minor.
   187	
   188	Read and analyze these images — run `ls -1 "$PWD"/slide-*.jpg` and use the exact absolute paths it prints:
   189	1. <absolute-path>/slide-N.jpg — (Expected: [brief description])
   190	2. <absolute-path>/slide-N.jpg — (Expected: [brief description])
   191	...
   192	
   193	Report ALL issues found, including minor ones.
   194	```
   195	
   196	### Verification Loop
   197	
   198	1. Generate slides → Convert to images → Inspect
   199	2. **List issues found** (if none found, look again more critically)
   200	3. Fix issues
   201	4. **Re-verify affected slides** — one fix often creates another problem
   202	5. Repeat until a full pass reveals no new issues
   203	
   204	**Do not declare success until you've completed at least one fix-and-verify cycle.**
   205	
   206	---
   207	
   208	## Converting to Images
   209	
   210	Convert presentations to individual slide images for visual inspection:
   211	
   212	```bash
   213	python scripts/office/soffice.py --headless --convert-to pdf output.pptx
   214	rm -f slide-*.jpg
   215	pdftoppm -jpeg -r 150 output.pdf slide
   216	ls -1 "$PWD"/slide-*.jpg
   217	```
   218	
   219	**Pass the absolute paths printed above directly to the view tool.** The `rm` clears stale images from prior runs. `pdftoppm` zero-pads based on page count: `slide-1.jpg` for decks under 10 pages, `slide-01.jpg` for 10-99, `slide-001.jpg` for 100+.
   220	
   221	**After fixes, rerun all four commands above** — the PDF must be regenerated from the edited `.pptx` before `pdftoppm` can reflect your changes.
   222	
   223	---
   224	
   225	## Dependencies
   226	
   227	- `pip install "markitdown[pptx]"` - text extraction
   228	- `pip install Pillow` - thumbnail grids
   229	- `npm install -g pptxgenjs` - creating from scratch
   230	- LibreOffice (`soffice`) - PDF conversion (auto-configured for sandboxed environments via `scripts/office/soffice.py`)
   231	- Poppler (`pdftoppm`) - PDF to images
